title: LLM-Augmented Stiefel Graph Neural Networks for Zero-Shot Spatio-temporal Forecasting_supp1-3689935.pdf
authors: Liang Xie
publish: N/A
abstract: Spatio-temporal time series (STTS) play a crucial role in domains such as traffic forecasting, energy scheduling, and financial analysis. However, accurate and efficient prediction remains challenging due to the complex dynamic dependencies across temporal and spatial dimensions. Existing Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) often struggle to balance effectiveness and efficiency when modeling dynamic spatio-temporal relations. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong capabilities in modeling long-range dependencies and generalization under few-shot and zero-shot conditions, yet their ability to capture spatio-temporal structures remains limited. To address this, we propose LAD-SGNN, a unified framework that integrates the advantages of graph-based and language-based modeling. Specifically, LAD-SGNN employs Spectral Graph Convolution on the Stiefel manifold (SGSC) together with Linear Dynamic Graph Optimization (LDGOSM) to efficiently extract dynamic spatio-temporal features with nearly linear complexity. Structured prompts and a spatio-temporal alignment mechanism are then designed to fuse the extracted dynamic spectral information with task semantics, which are fed into a lightweight LLM to achieve unified modeling of spatio-temporal structures and semantic reasoning. In this framework, SGSC efficiently represents dynamic spatial dependencies in the spectral domain, while the prompt-based alignment strategy explicitly injects spatio-temporal information into the LLM, thereby enhancing generalization across datasets. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world spatio-temporal datasets demonstrate that LAD-SGNN significantly improves prediction accuracy in both zero-shot and few-shot scenarios.

title: RTL to GDS Implementation of a RISC-V SoC with Integrated Timer using Openlane Framework
authors: Gavaskar K, Shivappriya S N, Ishwerya V, Kanagadharshan V, Elango S, Anbumani V
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Devices, Circuits, and Systems (ICDCS)
abstract: This paper describes the development and physical execution of a 32-bit RISC-V System-on-Chip (SoC) that incorporates a timer with programming unit utilizing a fully open-source RTL-to-GDSII design pipeline. Due to its open, modular, and license-free nature, the RISC-V architecture has emerged as a viable alternative to proprietary CPU designs. In order to solve these problems, a memory-mapped clock that can produce frequent signals for real-time management is combined with a tiny RISC-V core that supports the RV32I core instruction set. The entire SoC is built with the Open Lane streamlining structure and the Sky130 130 nm CMOS process design kit. Operational validity is tested using cycle-precise simulations, which ensure suitable command processing and dependable interrupt management. Logic synthesis generates an area-saving gate-level architecture translated to Sky130 standard cells. The final GDSII output shows that a fully operational RISC-V SoC can be built with only freeware tools, demonstrating the potential of open hardware design for embedded and industrial applications.

title: RTL to GDSII Implementation of Lightweight IOT Authentication Hardware using Multifunction Residue-Based Cryptographic Architectures
authors: Gavaskar K, Paramasivam K, Sneka S, Vimal R, Sandhyavathi K, Thilagavathy K P
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Devices, Circuits, and Systems (ICDCS)
abstract: Internet of Things (IOT) devices need to be secure and should be of low energy consumption. In terms of security, a lightweight hardware-based cryptography method is used. In the existing methods, it consumes more power due to continuous clock switching. This paper proposes a low-power hardware authentication encoder. In order to reduce dynamic power consumption due to unwanted clock switching in the system, clock gating is used at the architectural level. This clock gating uses an enable signal to reduce clock switching. This enable signal will be active only when necessary. This clock will propagate and reduce the power consumption in the system. The proposed design includes an ALU, a 63-bit LFSR, and a carry look-ahead adder. The design is verified, and it shows that the design uses low power consumption compared to other existing designs.

title: On the Availability Risks of Production LLM Services under Unbounded Inference_supp1-3689535.pdf
authors: Hongzi Zhu
publish: N/A
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become foundational components in a wide range of applications, including natural language understanding and generation, embodied intelligence, and scientific discovery. As their computational requirements continue to grow, these models are increasingly deployed as cloud-based services, allowing users to access powerful LLMs via the Internet. However, this deployment model introduces a new class of threat: denial-of-service (DoS) attacks via unbounded reasoning, where adversaries craft specially designed inputs that cause the model to enter excessively long or infinite generation loops. These attacks can exhaust backend compute resources, degrading or denying service to legitimate users. To mitigate such risks, many LLM providers adopt a closed-source, black box setting to obscure model internals. In this paper, we propose ThinkTrap, a novel input-space optimization framework for DoS attacks against LLM services even in black-box environments. The core idea of ThinkTrap is to first map discrete tokens into a continuous embedding space, then undertake efficient black-box optimization in a low-dimensional subspace exploiting input sparsity. The goal of this optimization is to identify adversarial prompts that induce extended or non-terminating generation across several state-of-the-art LLMs, achieving DoS with minimal token overhead. We evaluate ThinkTrap across multiple commercial, closed-source LLM services and observe that it can consistently induce abnormally long outputs and noticeable response-side degradation under black-box access. To further quantify the system-level impact of the attack, we conduct controlled experiments on private LLM deployments, where ThinkTrap reduces service throughput to as low as 1% of its original capacity and, in extreme cases, induces complete service failure due to resource exhaustion.

title: Impact Assessment of Structured Results for the Reliability of LLM-generated Tests
authors: Dovydas Marius Zapkus, Asta Slotkienė
publish: 2026 Open Conference of Electrical, Electronic and Information Sciences (eStream)
abstract: Unit testing remains a critically important, but labour-intensive process of software quality assurance. Large language models enable automated code generation, but their ability to generate reliable and effective test suites remains to be empirically evaluated. In this research, we suggest researching the reliability of LLMs for automated unit test generation, focusing on two quality metrics: syntactic correctness and fault-detection capabilities. We proposed an automated test-generation process using various source-code methods and, to ensure formatting consistency, used the Pydantic AI library to enforce a structured output format and return the generated unit tests as strictly typed data objects. Research focuses on the scarcely investigated area of strict LLM output structure validation, to identify whether the results obtained using this technique reached better-quality characteristics. The quality of the generated unit tests was evaluated using compilation metrics and mutation scores. The research results show a clear difference in the reliability of generated unit tests depending on LLMs. Although gpt-4.1 achieved the highest syntactic reliability with a 90.3% compilation success rate using focal methods, gemini-2.5-flash demonstrated a higher fault-detection capability. But the gemini-2.5-flash recorded the highest average mutation score of 93.23% (±11.74) and a median of 100%, outperforming gpt-4.1 and llama-3.3-70b-versatile models. This study confirms that LLMs can often generate successful unit tests, but when combined with structured output verification, such as with models such as gemini-2.5-flash, they enable the creation of reliable, robust software tests.

title: Development of the Fully Automatic Robotic Storyteller – Comparing Manual, Semi-Automatic, and LLM-Based Emotional Story Annotation for Multimodal Robotic Storytelling_supp1-3691816.pdf
authors: Sophia C. Steinhaeusser
publish: N/A
abstract: Storytelling has accompanied mankind since its beginnings and receiving stories is still a popular leisure activity. Today, new storytelling media are emerging. For example, social robots can act as storytellers, using different modalities such as facial expressions or body postures. With the goal of automating robotic storytelling, we annotated stories with emotion labels which a robot can use to automatically adapt its behavior to. Doing so, three different approaches and a baseline are compared in two studies in this paper: 1) manual labels by human annotators (MA), 2) software-based word-sensitive annotation using the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count program (LIWC), 3) a LLM approach using ChatGPT (GPT), and 4) random annotations as baseline (RBL). In an online study showing videos of a storytelling robot, the annotations were validated using a backwards annotation task, with GPT closely followed by MA achieving the best, and the RBL the worst results. In a subsequent lab user study, all versions of the story were compared regarding storytelling experience, emotions, and robot perception, revealing no significant differences for any of these variables based on the annotation approach, but a significant decrease of negative affect and fatigue over the storytelling in all conditions. Taken together, the results indicate suitability of both the manual and GPT approaches. On this empirical basis, the Automatic Robotic Storyteller was implemented using GPT-based annotations and bodily expressions. The reduced programming effort of our system makes robotic storytelling accessible to a broader audience, strengthening its deployment in the field of affective computing.

title: PRISM: Prompt Red-teaming and Injection Simulation for Models – A Scalable Framework for Evaluating LLM Robustness Against Malicious Prompts
authors: Nishith P, Niharika Panda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The rapid growth in the adoption of Large Language Models has brought with it some significant security vulnerabilities-one of which is prompt injection attacks. These attacks manipulate the behavior of LLMs using malicious user input, leading to the compromise of system integrity and the production of unintended or harmful output. Traditional detection approaches often cannot keep pace with the complexity and diversity of such attacks. We address this gap by presenting PRISM-Prompt Red-teaming and Injection Simulation for Models-a scalable framework designed to comprehensively assess the robustness of LLMs against adversarial prompts in a systematic manner. PRISM leverages a distributed computing architecture, utilizing Hadoop MapReduce and Ollama, to automatically assess LLM responses against a wide range of prompt variations, along with temperature configurations. It computes similarity-based measures from the large corpus of known prompt injection attempts that PRISM will process to describe model performance and identify successful attack vectors. The proposed work outlines the architecture of PRISM, reports on some initial exploratory data analysis of the HackAPrompt dataset, and demonstrates the ability of the framework to provide a complete, scalable, and automated solution for both LLM security auditing and red-teaming.

title: CoAnalyst: An Agentic, LLM-Based Cybersecurity Analyst for Industrial Control Systems
authors: Eren Çil, Jaafer Rahmani, Axel Sikora
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Industrial environments increasingly integrate operational technology (OT) with information technology (IT), expanding the cyber-attack surface and generating large volumes of heterogeneous security alerts. This complexity makes it difficult for analysts to triage and investigate threats across IT systems, Industrial IoT devices, and OT networks (e.g., industrial control system logs, protocol-level events such as Modbus traffic, and Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) alerts). In this paper, we present CoAnalyst, an “intelligent cybersecurity analyst” that acts as a security copilot to support human analysts. CoAnalyst is an agentic, OT-oriented framework that can be deployed at the edge on resource-constrained hardware while performing OT protocol-level alert correlation, enabling it to fuse and interpret alerts from multiple data sources close to where they are generated. It automates routine investigation steps and provides structured, easy-to-read contextual information. Its chat interface and agentic architecture enable dynamic toolcalling based on user intent, allowing adaptive investigation of OT networks, contextual reasoning over retrieved data, and ondemand threat analysis.

title: LLM-EmBEditor: Universal Base Editing Efficiency Prediction via Large Language Model Embeddings
authors: Lucas Schneider, Peter Minary

title: Auditable Drag and Drop AI Pipelines: OCR LLM Composition with Rubric Scoring, HITL, and Provenance First MLOps
authors: K Ravishankar, Mithran M. V., B. K. Prethika
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: We present an auditable, drag-and-drop platform for composing OCR–LLM pipelines with explainability, rubric-based scoring, human-in-the-loop (HITL) review, and provenance-first MLOps. Blocks encapsulate OCR detectors/recognizers, promp table LLM extractors, XAI widgets, scoring policies, and review queues. A Node-RED-style orchestrator records configuration, model/version, inputs/outputs, and human actions to a relational store for replay and audit. We formalize rubric scoring as a constrained, weighted objective and pair it with explanations (Grad-CAM/Score-CAM overlays, token-level rationales) to justify decisions. HITL patterns (escalation, adjudication, exception) close the loop for reliability and compliance. On ICDAR SROIE (receipts) and FUNSD (forms) we report OCR/KIE F1, end-to-end latency, reviewer time per operation, and provenance coverage. Results show competitive accuracy with traceable decision trails, offering a practical template for trustworthy, policy-constrained document AI in production.

title: The Silent Scholar Problem: A Probabilistic Framework for Breaking Epistemic Asymmetry in LLM Agents
authors: Zan-Kai Chong, Hiroyuki Ohsaki, Bryan Ng
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Robotics and Technologies for Industrial Automation (ROBOTHIA)
abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs), often enhanced by retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), are powerful processors of digital content. However, RAG is limited to consumption, constraining agents as unidirectional consumers. We term this limitation epistemic asymmetry. This imbalance creates a globally inefficient landscape, where silent scholars redundantly reconstruct similar reasoning in isolation, stagnating collective intelligence. To break this asymmetry, we propose a formal probabilistic framework that provides agents with a non-altruistic motive to engage in bidirectional knowledge exchange. We model an agent's belief in a proposition as a BetaBernoulli distribution with a forgetting factor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\gamma)$</tex>. This allows us to isolate epistemic uncertainty as the variance of belief, establishing a dual motive for interaction: (1) a homeostatic drive to maintain certainty against temporal decay, and (2) an optimal learning strategy that targets maximum ambiguity <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{E}[\theta]=0.5)$</tex>. Furthermore, we address the practical challenges of deployment by introducing epistemic caching, a mechanism that leverages the forgetting factor to dynamically allocate resources to the non-stationary active head of the knowledge distribution. While our current analysis focuses on independent propositions and binary feedback to establish a baseline for this motive, we discuss how the framework extends to correlated and multi-categorical knowledge structures. Finally, we discuss how these accumulated belief states can serve as verifiable reward signals for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and high-quality data filters for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), effectively bridging the gap between inference-time interaction and long-term model alignment. Simulation results validate this architecture, demonstrating that our uncertainty-driven strategy significantly outperforms random baselines in heterogeneous (Zipfian) environments, successfully prioritising the active head of the knowledge distribution while maintaining adaptability to concept drift.

title: Enterprise Secure AI Execution Engine: A Zero-Trust Security Gateway for Enterprise LLM Systems
authors: Kandula Krishna Chaitanya Reddy, Tiriveedhi Chandra Lekha, Thrisha P, Medam Arjun, K Radhika, P Naresh
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Organizations are increasingly adopting Large Language Models (LLMs) for automated decision-making and employee support and secure handling of sensitive organizational data. The systems enabled by these technologies deliver substantial business productivity gains. The systems create major security problems because their implementation enables users to conduct prompt injection attacks and data exfiltration and privilege escalation and adversarial manipulation and policy override attacks. LLM systems operate through natural language interactions which create particular security problems because standard security methods cannot stop attackers who use designed prompts to access LLM systems. The paper introduces an Enterprise Secure AI Execution Engine which functions as a Zero-Trust AI Security Gateway that protects enterprise LLM deployments through its security framework. The proposed architecture combines multiple security mechanisms which include Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Data Loss Prevention (DLP) and adversarial prompt detection and deterministic tokenization to create a security system that enforces stringent access protection rules. The system considers all incoming user data to be untrusted and it requires several security checks before permitting model operation. The system demonstrated its capability to identify adversarial prompts while safeguarding sensitive data with minimal effect on processing efficiency. The results demonstrate the system effectively blocks major security threats while it maintains strong defenses against unauthorized data extraction attempts. The proposed framework establishes a secure deployment architecture for enterprise LLM systems and it works best for organizations that need to protect their most sensitive operations.

title: Optimized MS-SIDM: A Context-Aware Sensitive Information Detection Framework via LLM Reasoning and Non-Linear Risk Quantification
authors: Hang Li, Yuechun Feng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The exponential growth of multi-source data aggregation has exacerbated sensitive information leakage risks, challenging existing privacy compliance frameworks. Traditional detection methods, such as BERT-based NER, often suffer from ‘‘contextual blindness’’ and fail to address the ‘‘Mosaic Effect’’—where non-linear risk accumulation occurs across heterogeneous data streams. To address these deficiencies, we propose the Optimized Multi-Source Sensitive Information Detection Model (MS-SIDM). Our approach employs Qwen2.5-7B as a semantic backbone, fine-tuned via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a taxonomy-guided adversarial dataset to distinguish authentic leaks from high-entropy traps. Furthermore, we introduce a Non-Linear Risk Quantification Mechanism integrating a Square Root Scale Factor (Mi) and a Density Compensation Factor (Di), alongside a Dual-Risk Perception mechanism (Eenv). This framework enables contextual threat assessment to dynamically calibrate risk scores against multi-source collusion. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that MS-SIDM significantly outperforms SOTA baselines (e.g., RoBERTa, Llama 2), achieving an F1-score of 97.46% and a Hard-Exact Match (EM) accuracy of 91.60% under adversarial stress. By synthesizing LLM-based semantic reasoning with a rigorous mathematical risk framework, this study demonstrates strong potential as a solution for asynchronous privacy monitoring and automated compliance auditing, subject to further validation in active production settings.

title: AI Against AI: A Text-Vectorization-Driven Framework for Detecting LLM-Generated Phishing Emails using Classical ML and Deep Learning
authors: Veldin A. Talorete, Ariel Roy L. Reyes, Javer C. Borngo
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made phishing emails much more complex, providing attackers with an unprecedented opportunity to create message forms with fluency and context awareness that can be much harder to distinguish from genuine correspondence. This paper introduces AI Against AI, a text-vectorization-based methodology for identifying phishing emails produced by LLM. utilizing machine learning techniques in conjunction with a balanced dataset of 4,000 emails that included human-written legitimate emails, human-written phishing emails, AI-generated legitimate emails, and AI-generated phishing emails. However, the study assesses a number of classifiers, including Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Convolutional Neural Networks, in addition to three text-representation techniques, TF-IDF, Word2Vec, and BERT. Its results show that text representation has a significant impact on performance, far more so than classification selection. TF-IDF combined with SVM and XGBoost achieved an accuracy up to 0.99, while Word2Vec integrated with CNN also achieved an accuracy of 0.99. BERT showed the most stable performance over various classifiers, although it requires more computation. In summary, this study's contribution satisfies the requirement for AI-aware phishing detection systems that can effectively thwart the increasingly convincing phishing content produced by modern language models.

title: Web Image Collection and LLM-Based Labeling to Provide Dynamic References for Visual Inspection of Drug Packaging
authors: Yona Zakaria, Chouman Hussein, Rui Ishiyama, Eiki Ishidera, Tomokazu Matsui, Hirohiko Suwa, Keiichi Yasumoto
publish: 2026 IIEEJ International Conference on Image Electronics and Visual Computing (IEVC)
abstract: Reliable reference images of drug packaging are essential for visual inspection, a widely recommended first step in identifying counterfeit medicines. However, maintaining comprehensive and up-to-date reference databases is challenging, as packaging designs change frequently and new counterfeit variants continually emerge, making manual curation laborintensive and not scalable in practice. This paper presents a Web Intelligence system that addresses this challenge by automatically collecting packaging images from the web to provide dynamic reference information for visual inspection. The system integrates (1) web image crawling to retrieve relevant packaging variants, (2) feature-point matching to filter visually irrelevant results, and (3) large language model (LLM) analysis of webpage content to infer context and assign authenticity labels. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework can automatically collect visually consistent packaging images and assign soft authenticity cues derived from the text of the associated webpage to support visual inspection in detecting counterfeit packaging.
